title: Sticky toffee traybake
subtitle: The pub pudding as a slab cake, served with toffee sauce
excerpt: A 30 × 20 cm sticky toffee sponge: Medjool dates soaked in boiling water and blended into the batter, bicarbonate of soda providing the rise, dark muscovado sugar for depth. The toffee sauce goes on hot. Serves 15.
sourceNotes: Sticky toffee pudding is a British pudding documented in the lake district cooking tradition from the mid-twentieth century. Dates in British baking are documented in Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, in the section on fruit cakes and date loaves; the soft-cooked date approach (soaking in boiling water to soften) is consistent with Beeton's fruit-soaking methods. The traybake format rather than individual pudding basins is a practical adaptation; the composition of dates, muscovado sugar, bicarbonate of soda, and butter follows the original pub-kitchen tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the un-sauced sponge cut into portions. Defrost at room temperature and warm with fresh toffee sauce.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sponge bakes 2 days ahead. Make the toffee sauce fresh and pour over warm sponge just before serving.

recipeTools.notes: 30 × 20 cm traybake tin, greased and lined.
Or a fork and patience — the dates need to be mostly smooth.

glossaryTerms.definition: Medjool dates soaked in boiling water until soft, then blended or mashed into a paste. The purée adds intense sweetness, moisture, and depth to baked goods without dry texture or visible pieces. The soaking water goes into the batter too, carrying the date's natural sugars.

content.text: Sticky toffee pudding in a traybake is the pub classic made portable. The sponge is the same: dates soaked in boiling water and blended into the batter with bicarbonate of soda, which reacts with the date's acidity to give a light, tender crumb despite how dense the raw batter looks. The dark muscovado sugar provides the caramel depth. The toffee sauce of butter, cream, and dark sugar goes on top immediately after baking, while the sponge is still hot enough to absorb it into the surface.

is the key ingredient. Medjool dates are the most flavourful; dried pitted dates work but produce a slightly more fibrous result. Blend them with their soaking water until mostly smooth — a few small pieces are fine and add texture.

items.prepNote: 1.5 teaspoons — stir into the hot date water

content.text: Place the pitted {{dates-medjool}} Medjool dates in a bowl. Pour 200 ml of boiling water over them and stir in the {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}. The bicarbonate will foam slightly — this is normal and activates when the purée meets the batter. Leave for 10 minutes to soften, then blend with a stick blender or mash with a fork until mostly smooth. The mixture should look like a thick, dark purée.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Grease and line the traybake tin. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} softened butter and {{muscovado-dark}} dark muscovado sugar together until combined — this won't cream as pale as caster sugar; muscovado stays darker and slightly grainy, which is normal. Beat in the {{eggs}} eggs one at a time. Fold in the {{plain-flour}} and {{baking-powder}} sifted together. Add the date purée and {{vanilla-extract}} vanilla extract and fold to combine. The batter will be fairly thin.

Pour the batter into the prepared tin and bake for 30–35 minutes, until the sponge is set — it will feel firm when pressed in the centre and a skewer through the middle will come out clean. The surface will be dark from the muscovado; this is not a sign of burning. Leave the sponge in the tin.
Make and pour the toffee sauce

While the sponge bakes, combine the {{unsalted-butter}}, {{muscovado-dark}} for the sauce, {{double-cream}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a small saucepan. Heat over medium heat, stirring, until the butter melts and the sugar dissolves. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook for 3 minutes, stirring, until the sauce has thickened slightly and coats the back of a spoon. Pour the hot toffee sauce over the hot sponge immediately after it comes out of the oven. Leave to absorb and cool for 15 minutes before cutting into portions.
